content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Pract:icès Commission
January 19,
1994
Frank L.
Stoner
1312
W.
Tenth Street
Antioch,
CA
94509
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—93-399
Dear Mr.
Stone:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) .‘/
The Commission does
not provide advice concerning past conduct.
(Regulation
18329(b) (8) (A).)
Accordingly,
we can provide only the following
informal response to your letter.
2
!
QUESTION
What are the mass mailing identification requirements when an
entity,
such as a local chamber of commerce,
includes a
committee’s political flyer in its monthly newsletter to its
members?
CONCLUSION
When political materials are included along with non
political materials in an entity’s routine mailing, it is
sufficient that the entity’s name and address appear on the
outside of the mailing.
Political materials produced for
inclusion in such a mailing must contain the name and address of
the candidate or committee which paid for them.
i_/’
Govermuent Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 j Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0S07
•
(916) 322-566(1


Frank L.
Stone
Our File No.
1-93—399
Page
2
FACTS
You represent a committee formed to oppose the qualification
of a recall in the City of Antioch.
The committee produced a
flyer opposing the recall which it intended to use as a handout
and as an insert in the local newspapers.
It was not intended
that the flyer would be mailed.
After the flyer was printed, the
local Chamber of Commerce requested approximately 650 copies for
insertion in its monthly newsletter to its members.
The Chamber’s
name and address were printed on the outside of the newsletter.
The flyer contained the name of the committee and indicated that
the committee’s identification number was pending from the
Secretary of State.
ANALYSIS
Section 84305 requires that any “mass mailing”
(over 200
pieces of mail sent in a calendar month)
sent by a candidate or
committee must contain,
on the outside of the mailing, the name
and address of the sender in no less than 6—point type.
The
“sender” of a mass mailing is the candidate or committee that pays
for or incurs the largest portion of expenses attributable to
designing, printing or posting the mailing.
(Regulation 18435.)
The Act’s mass mailing provisions do not specifically address
your situation.
The Commission has previously advised that when
political materials are included along with non-political
materials in an entity’s routine mailings, the mass mailing
identification need only appear on the political materials and
need not be included on the outside of the mailing.
(Bagatelos
Advice Letter,
No.
I-88-383.)
Therefore, it is sufficient to
include only the Chamber’s name and address on the outside of its
regular newsletter even if the newsletter contains political
materials.
With regard to the political materials,
inclusion of
the committee’s name and address on those materials will ensure
full compliance with the requirements of the Act.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
met at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
